summary:
The Defenestration of Prague (May 23, 1618 AD) was the event that triggered the [[event:2328|Thirty Years' War]], when a group of Protestant Bohemian nobles threw three Catholic Habsburg officials — the royal governors William Slavata, Jaroslav Bořita of Martinic, and their secretary Philip Fabricius — from the windows of Prague Castle's Hradčany complex, a drop of approximately 15–21 meters. The immediate cause was a dispute over whether the new Catholic Habsburg King Ferdinand II (about to become Holy Roman Emperor) had violated the Letter of Majesty (1609) that had granted Bohemian Protestants religious freedoms. The Protestant nobles who threw the officials framed the act in legal terms, claiming it as a traditional Bohemian legal remedy for violations of privilege — defenestration as juridical process.

Somewhat farcically, all three officials survived the fall, landing in a heap of garbage or a dung heap below the window (they had to leave Prague quickly). The Catholic interpretation was miraculous divine intervention; the Protestant interpretation was that God had proved they weren't worth saving. The event triggered the Bohemian revolt: the Protestant Bohemian Estates deposed Ferdinand II and elected the German Protestant Calvinist Frederick V ('the Winter King') as King of Bohemia in his place. Ferdinand's response was crushing: at the [[event:2404|Battle of White Mountain]] (November 1620), Frederick's army was destroyed in less than an hour, the Bohemian revolt was suppressed, and Ferdinand used the victory to permanently abolish Bohemian Protestant rights and Bohemian political autonomy. The rebellion's failure pushed the conflict onto a larger European stage, as Protestant powers rallied against Habsburg hegemony.
